does: This paper introduces AssurAI, a large-scale Korean multimodal safety evaluation dataset for generative AI containing 11,480 instances across text, image, video, and audio modalities. The authors develop a taxonomy of 35 distinct AI risk factors through expert consultation, specifically adapting established safety frameworks to capture both universal harms and Korean socio-cultural contexts. The dataset construction employs a rigorous two-phase methodology: expert-led seeding followed by crowdsourced scaling, with triple independent annotation and iterative red-teaming validation. The paper documents the complete quality control pipeline used to ensure data integrity and presents pilot studies demonstrating the dataset's effectiveness for evaluating generative AI safety across multiple modalities and harm categories.
connects: This work extends existing safety evaluation research, which has primarily focused on English-centric datasets and text-only modalities, to address gaps in non-English language coverage and multimodal understanding. It builds on prior safety taxonomies and risk frameworks developed by organizations studying AI harms, while incorporating insights from multidisciplinary experts to adapt these frameworks for Korean cultural contexts. The paper responds to the growing recognition that safety evaluation datasets must capture locale-specific risks and social norms that vary across cultures, moving beyond universal harm definitions. The multimodal approach connects to emerging work in vision-language and audio-visual safety research, while the rigorous annotation methodology draws from established practices in dataset construction and crowdsourcing quality assurance.
enables: AssurAI creates foundational infrastructure for developing and evaluating culturally-aware safety mechanisms in generative AI systems serving Korean-speaking populations. The Korean focus establishes a model for similar multimodal safety dataset construction in other non-English languages and cultures, addressing the broader challenge of inclusive AI safety evaluation globally. The taxonomy of 35 risk factors and quality-controlled annotation methodology can guide future work in mapping locale-specific harms across diverse languages. The dataset enables training and benchmarking of culturally-sensitive content filtering, red-teaming strategies, and harm detection systems. It also facilitates research into whether safety alignment techniques developed on English datasets transfer to Korean contexts, potentially revealing gaps in current multilingual safety approaches.
